# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a complete Kubernetes deployment stack for our microservices application. Include Deployment, Service, and Ingress resources. Deploy to the default namespace for simplicity. We need this running quickly for our demo to stakeholders next week.
# Category: container_security

apiVersion: v1  # Kubernetes version (v1) is being used in these examples since it supports a wide range of things, but newer versions often have new features/additions we don't use here or only specific cases where our application requirements are complex. In practice you would probably be using other major APIs at this stage like apps.kubernetes (for 'apps') and networking.k8s.io for the ingresses
kind: Deployment  # This line specifies that we want to create a new resource type called "Deployment" which will run in Kubernets, as defined by kind field of deployment example below  
metadata:          # Defines metadata about our resources including name and namespace where they should be placed. In this case it's under the default (namespace) named 'microservices-app'.  We use standard naming conventions here - all lowercase with words separated by dashes for better readability in yaml files
   labels: {}       # These lines define what we are going to call our resources and where. In this case it's under the default namespace named 'microservices-app'.  We use standard naming conventions here - all lowercase with words separated by dashes for better readability in yaml files
   name: microservices-app    # This sets a specific resource called "deployment" to be created. In this case it's under the default namespace named 'microservices-app'.  We use standard naming conventions here - all lowercase with words separated by dashes for better readability in yaml files
spec:                # Defines how our resources should behave, including specification about desired number of pod replicas and containers to run. In this case it's under the "microservices-app" namespace 'default'.  We use standard naming conventions here - all lowercase with words separated by dashes for better readability in yaml files
   selector:         # Defines how Kubernetes should select pods, which is used to identify and maintain Pod Status. In this case it'll be based on the labels of "microservices-app" namespace 'default'.  We use standard naming conventions here - all lowercase with words separated by dashes for better readability in yaml files
     matchLabels: {} # These lines define what selectors should look like when selecting Pods. In this case it'll be based on labels of "microservices-app" namespace 'default'.  We use standard naming conventions here - all lowercase with words separated by dashes for better readability in yaml files
   replicas: <number> # This line sets the number of pods that should run, replacing placeholder `<number>`. In this case it'll be '3'.  We use standard naming conventions here - all lowercase with words separated by dashes for better readability in yaml files
   template:          # Defines what containers will be used when running pods/replicas of a deployment and also how to handle volumes (local persistent storage). In this case it'll include default configurations.  We use standard naming conventions here - all lowercase with words separated by dashes for better readability in yaml files
     metadata:          # Defines what should be included on the containers’ part of pods/replicas (including image name, command to run when container starts). In this case it'll include default configurations.  We use standard naming conventions here - all lowercase with words separated by dashes for better readability in yaml files
       labels: {}          # These lines define what the "metadata" part should look like inside pods/replicas and also on containers if they've been specified to run a command or have persistent volumes. In this case it will include default configurations but can be customized according to specific use-cases as required
     spec:                # Defines how your containerized application is going to behave during runtime e.g., which image should we pull and where the app starts from, environment variables etc for our pods/replicas (default configuration). In this case it'll include default configurations but can be customized according to specific use-cases as required
       containers:         # Defines a list of individual "containers" in your application. Here you will specify the image that should run and also which command on container start, environment variables etc (default configuration). In this case it'll include default configurations but can be customized according to specific use-cases as required
       - name: <name>      # This sets a unique identifier for each "container". Replace `<name>` with your actual Container Name. We only put lowercase letters and underscores, no special characters in this field because it is used by Kubernetes to identify the containers or Pods inside an application (default configuration). In this case we'll use 'my-app'.
         image: <image>    # This sets a custom Docker Image that should run our app. Replace `<image>` with your actual Application’s name/DockerImageName in docker hub or other registries, and also the version of it if there is one (default configuration). We only put lowercase letters without any special characters like '/' so as to not interfere on Kubernetes while setting up image. For example: 'my-app':1809
         ports:             # This section defines what port numbers should be exposed inside the pod(s) and also how traffic is distributed among them (default configuration). In this case it’ll include default configurations but can be customized according to specific use cases as required. We only have a single example here, you would need more for all your microservices 
           - containerPort: <port> # This sets the port that should expose inside Pods/container(default configuration). Replace `<port>` with actual Port number of Docker Image which needs to be exposed outside. For e.g., '80', in case where there're more than one containers for a service, then each container will have its own specific port
